If we carefully consider the golden rule of “doing to others as we would they should do unto us,” many specific instructions might be left out. We can have no ownership in anything that we find. Religion teaches us to be friendly and ready to do all good things for everyone. We do not know how soon we may need help.

God’s providence extends to the smallest matters, and his commands are such that even in them we may be in the fear of the Lord, as we are under his eye and care. However, the effect of these laws, which seem small, is such that when found among the things of God's law, they are to be considered great things. If we want to prove ourselves to be God’s people, we must have respect for his will and for his glory, and not for the empty trends of the world. Even in putting on our clothes, as in eating or drinking, all must be done with a serious regard to preserve our own and others' purity in heart and actions. Our eye should be single, our heart simple, and our behavior consistent.

These and similar regulations might have been necessary then, and yet it is not necessary that we should carefully examine regarding them. The laws relate to the seventh commandment, laying a restraint upon fleshly lusts which war against the soul.
